title: Press Release: Housing Price Growth Expectations Increase; Renters Express Concern for Ability to Own a Home

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: The Federal Reserve Bank of New York released results from its 2024 SCE Housing Survey, showing that average one-year-ahead home price growth expectations increased to 5.1% in February 2024 from 2.6% a year prior, the second highest reading in survey history. Rent price growth expectations also increased to 9.7%, the second highest since 2022, while households expect mortgage rates to rise further to 8.7% in one year and 9.7% in three years, both series highs.
key_points: Average one-year-ahead home price growth expectations increased to 5.1% in February 2024 from 2.6% in February 2023, slightly above the pre-pandemic mean of 4.2% but below the 2022 series high of 7%
Rent price growth expectations for the next year increased by 1.5 percentage points to 9.7%, the second highest reading since 2022
Households expect mortgage rates to rise to 8.7% one year from now and 9.7% in three years, both series highs; however, 61% believe rates will fall over the next 12 months
74.2% of renters stated obtaining a mortgage is somewhat or very difficult, an 8.4 percentage point increase from the prior year and well above the 2021 low of 50.5%
Renters' self-assessed probability of ever owning a home decreased to 40.1%, a new series low
Expected residential mobility fell to series lows at both one-year (13.4%) and three-year (24.5%) horizons, continuing a declining trend since 2014
why_it_matters: This survey provides critical inflation expectations data for the housing sector, a major component of CPI and PCE calculations. The reacceleration in home and rent price expectations suggests shelter inflation may remain elevated, potentially complicating Federal Reserve monetary policy decisions. The record-low mobility and high mortgage accessibility concerns indicate structural constraints in the housing market that could sustain price pressures.
macro_context: The survey data indicates elevated housing-related inflation expectations that could influence Federal Reserve policy deliberations. One-year-ahead rent expectations at 9.7% and home price expectations at 5.1% suggest continued shelter inflation pressures, which typically lag market rates. The record-high mortgage rate expectations and declining homeownership probability indicate affordability constraints that may suppress demand but not prices in the near term.
important_numbers: 8.7% expected mortgage rate one year ahead
9.7% expected mortgage rate three years ahead
74.2% of renters finding mortgage access difficult
40.1% renters' self-assessed probability of ever owning a home
notes: Source is a Federal Reserve Bank of New York press release presenting 2024 SCE Housing Survey results. While not a direct monetary policy action, the survey provides relevant macro data on housing inflation expectations and credit conditions that inform Fed policy considerations. The approximately two-year gap between publication (May 2024) and TODAY (April 2026) makes this a historical record; some findings may have evolved. Survey fielded in February 2024. Relevant to macro catalysts through shelter inflation expectations and housing affordability indicators.
